translator: Public Domain Translation by Robot Mason
1.1: Now (atha), the instruction (anuśāsana) on Yoga begins.
1.2: Yoga is the cessation (nirodhaḥ) of the fluctuations (vṛtti) of the mind-field (citta).
1.3: Then the Seer (draṣṭṛ) abides (avasthānam) in its own true nature (svarūpa).
1.4: At other times, there is identification with the fluctuations (vṛtti-sārūpya).
1.5: The fluctuations (vṛttayaḥ) are five, and they are painful (kliṣṭa) or not painful (akliṣṭa).
1.6: They are right knowledge (pramāṇa), wrong knowledge (viparyaya), imagination/fancy (vikalpa), sleep (nidrā), and memory (smṛti).
1.7: Right knowledge (pramāṇa) arises from direct perception (pratyakṣa), inference (anumāna), and reliable testimony (āgama).
1.8: Wrong knowledge (viparyaya) is false cognition not based on the thing as it is.
1.9: Imagination (vikalpa) follows after words and ideas devoid of a corresponding object.
1.10: Sleep (nidrā) is a fluctuation grounded on the experience of absence (abhāva-pratyaya).
1.11: Memory (smṛti) is the retention of experienced objects, not letting them go.
1.12: Their cessation is brought about by practice (abhyāsa) and dispassion (vairāgya).
1.13: Practice (abhyāsa) is the effort to remain established there (tatra sthitau yatnaḥ).
1.14: It becomes firmly grounded when done for a long time (dīrgha-kāla), without interruption (nairantarya), and with sincere devotion (satkāra-āsevita).
1.15: Dispassion (vairāgya) is mastery over craving for objects seen or heard about (dṛṣṭānuśravika-viṣaya).
1.16: The higher dispassion is indifference to even the constituents (guṇa: sattva, rajas, tamas) through knowledge of the Self (puruṣa-khyāti).
1.17: Cognitive absorption (samprajñāta-samādhi) is accompanied by reasoning (vitarka), reflection (vicāra), bliss (ānanda), and sense of I-ness (asmitā).
1.18: Beyond that is noncognitive absorption (asamprajñāta-samādhi), where only latent impressions (saṁskāra) remain after cessation (nirodha).
1.19: Some attain that state through birth or nature’s dissolution (prakṛty-apūra), their samādhi being innate.
1.20: For others, it is preceded by faith/confidence (śraddhā), energy (vīrya), mindfulness/memory (smṛti), absorption (samādhi), and insight (prajñā).
1.21: For those with intense ardor (tīvra-saṁvegānām), realization is near.
1.22: Even among them there is a threefold distinction: mild, medium, or intense (mṛdu, madhya, adhimātra).
1.23: Or by devotion (praṇidhāna) to Īśvara (a special Purusha).
1.24: Īśvara is a special Self (puruṣa-viśeṣa), untouched by afflictions (kleśa), actions (karma), their fruits (vipāka), and latent seeds (āśaya).
1.25: In Him, the seed of omniscience (sarvajña-bīja) is unsurpassed.
1.26: He is the teacher of even the ancients (pūrveṣām api guruḥ), not limited by time.
1.27: His designator (vācaka) is the syllable Om (praṇava).
1.28: Recite (japa) Om and contemplate its meaning (artha-bhāvanā).
1.29: From that practice, inwardness (pratyak-cetanā) arises and obstacles (antarāya) fall away.
1.30: Disease, apathy, doubt, carelessness, laziness, sense-indulgence, false views, non-attainment, and instability—these nine are the distractions (antarāyāḥ) of the mind.
1.31: With distraction come suffering (duḥkha), depression (daurmanasya), bodily tremor (aṅgamejayatva), and disturbed breathing (śvāsa-praśvāsa).
1.32: To counter them, practice one principle (eka-tattvābhyāsa).
1.33: By cultivating friendliness (maitrī) toward the happy, compassion (karuṇā) for the suffering, joy (muditā) in the virtuous, and equanimity (upekṣā) toward the non-virtuous, the mind becomes clear (citta-prasādanam).
1.34: Or by exhalation and retention of the breath (pracchardana-vidhāraṇa).
1.35: Or by steadying attention on a sense-object that brings stability.
1.36: Or on a sorrowless, radiant inner light (viśokā vā jyotiṣmatī).
1.37: Or on a mind free from attachment (vīta-rāga-viṣaya).
1.38: Or on knowledge from dreams or dreamless sleep (svapna-nidrā-jñāna).
1.39: Or by meditation on anything one finds elevating (yathābhimata-dhyānāt).
1.40: Mastery extends from the smallest atom (aṇu) to the greatest (mahattva).
1.41: When fluctuations are thinned, the mind is like a transparent jewel (abhi-jāta), reflecting the knower, the knowing, or the known—this is absorption (samāpatti).
1.42: In reasoning absorption (savitaraka-samāpatti), there is conceptualization with word, meaning, and knowledge mixed together.
1.43: When memory is purified, the mind is emptied of its own nature; resting only on the object—this is non-reasoning absorption (nirvitarka).
1.44: Likewise with subtler objects: reflective (savichāra) and non-reflective (nirvichāra) absorptions.
1.45: The subtlest object is the unmanifest (aliṅga) of nature (prakṛti).
1.46: These absorptions with seed (sabīja) are called cognitive (samprajñāta).
1.47: From clarity in non-reflective absorption (nirvichāra-prasāda) arises the light of the Self.
1.48: There arises truth-bearing knowledge (ṛtambharā prajñā) that accords with reality.
1.49: It differs from inference and testimony because it is of particulars (viśeṣa-artha).
1.50: The impression (saṁskāra) born of that knowledge suppresses other impressions.
1.51: With restraint (nirodha) even of that impression, seedless absorption (nirbīja-samādhi) dawns.
2.1: Austerity (tapas), self-study/recitation (svādhyāya), and devotion to Īśvara (Īśvara-praṇidhāna) constitute Kriyā-Yoga (the Yoga of action).
2.2: Its purpose is to attenuate afflictions (kleśa) and bring about samādhi.
2.3: The afflictions (kleśāḥ) are ignorance (avidyā), egoism (asmitā), attachment (rāga), aversion (dveṣa), and clinging to life (abhiniveśa).
2.4: Ignorance (avidyā) is the breeding ground for the others—whether dormant, weakened, interrupted, or fully active.
2.5: Ignorance is to regard the impermanent as permanent, impure as pure, painful as pleasant, and the non-Self (anātman) as the Self (ātman).
2.6: Egoism (asmitā) is the identification of the Seer (draṣṭṛ) with the instruments of seeing (buddhi/manas).
2.9: Clinging to life (abhiniveśa) is self-perpetuating, even in the wise; it flows on by its own force.
2.10: In their subtle form, the afflictions are to be resolved back into their source (pratiprasava).
2.11: In their gross form they are to be eliminated by meditation (dhyāna).
2.12: Actions (karma) and their fruits (vipāka), rooted in afflictions, produce experiences across lifetimes, circumstances, and tendencies.
2.13: So long as the root exists, they fructify as birth, life-span, and experience.
2.14: Because of the changing guṇas (sattva-rajas-tamas) and conflicting tendencies, worldly experiences are painful.
2.15: Hence, for the discerning (vivekin), everything is suffering (sarvaṁ duḥkham) due to change, anxiety, latent impressions, and the activities of the guṇas.
2.16: Suffering that has not yet arisen is to be avoided (heya).
2.17: The conjunction (saṁyoga) of the Seer (puruṣa) and the seen (dṛśya) is the cause of suffering.
2.18: The seen (dṛśya) consists of the guṇas—illumination (sattva), activity (rajas), inertia (tamas)—forming elements, senses, and mind; it exists for experience (bhoga) and liberation (apavarga).
2.19: The stages of the guṇas are: specific (viśeṣa), non-specific (aviśeṣa), the mere sign (liṅga-mātra), and the unmanifest (aliṅga).
2.20: The Seer (draṣṭṛ, puruṣa) is pure seeing, though it appears to see via the mind (citta).
2.21: The seen exists only for the Seer.
2.22: Though it ceases for the liberated, it continues for others as a common field.
2.23: Their conjunction is for the Seer’s recognition of its own powers (sva-śakti).
2.24: Ignorance (avidyā) is the cause of that conjunction.
2.25: With the removal of ignorance, the conjunction ends. This is isolation/liberation (kaivalya): the Seer stands alone.
2.26: Uninterrupted discriminative knowledge (viveka-khyāti) is the means to its cessation.
2.27: In one who has that highest insight, wisdom progresses in seven stages (or limbs).
2.28: By practicing the limbs (aṅga) of Yoga, impurities are destroyed and the light of knowledge dawns to discriminative insight.
2.29: The eight limbs (aṣṭāṅga) are: restraints (yama), observances (niyama), posture (āsana), breath regulation (prāṇāyāma), sense withdrawal (pratyāhāra), concentration (dhāraṇā), meditation (dhyāna), and absorption (samādhi).
2.30: The restraints (yama) are non-violence (ahiṁsā), truthfulness (satya), non-stealing (asteya), continence (brahmacarya), and non-greed (aparigraha).
2.31: These are the great vow (mahā-vrata), universal and unrestricted by class, place, time, or duty.
2.32: The observances (niyama) are purity (śauca), contentment (santoṣa), austerity (tapas), self-study (svādhyāya), and devotion to Īśvara (Īśvara-praṇidhāna).
2.33: When disturbed by unwholesome thoughts (vitarkāḥ), cultivate their opposites (pratipakṣa-bhāvanā).
2.34: Unwholesome thoughts—violence and the rest—whether done, caused, or approved, arise from greed, anger, or delusion, and may be mild, medium, or intense; they lead to endless suffering and ignorance. Oppose them by their contrary.
2.35: In the presence of one established in non-violence (ahiṁsā), enmity ceases.
2.36: When truthfulness (satya) is established, actions bear their fruits in accord.
2.37: When non-stealing (asteya) is established, all jewels come (i.e., unearned wealth does not attract).
2.38: When continence (brahmacarya) is established, vigor (vīrya) is gained.
2.39: From non-greed (aparigraha) comes knowledge of the why and how of past and future births.
2.40: From purity (śauca) arises disgust for one’s own body and separation from others’ bodies (i.e., non-fascination).
2.41: From purity also come cheerfulness, one-pointedness, mastery of the senses, and fitness for Self-realization.
2.43: From austerity (tapas), impurities are burned and the body and senses attain perfection.
2.44: From self-study (svādhyāya), communion with the desired deity (iṣṭa-devatā-samprayoga).
2.47: It is mastered by relaxing effort (prayatna-śaithilya) and meditation on the infinite (ananta-samāpatti).
2.48: Then the pairs of opposites (dvandva) no longer disturb.
2.49: After posture is mastered, breath regulation (prāṇāyāma) is the pause of inhalation and exhalation.
2.50: It may be external, internal, or suspended; measured by place, time, and number; prolonged and subtle.
2.51: The fourth kind transcends the sphere of inhalation and exhalation (bāhya/ābhyantara-viṣaya-ākṣepi).
2.52: From that practice the covering over the inner light (pracchanda-āvṛta) is thinned.
2.53: And the mind becomes fit for concentration (dhāraṇā).
2.54: Sense withdrawal (pratyāhāra) is as if the senses imitate the mind by disengaging from their objects.
2.55: Then comes supreme mastery over the senses (paramā vaśyatā).
3.1: Concentration (dhāraṇā) is fixing the mind (citta) on one place (deśa).
3.2: Meditation (dhyāna) is the continuous flow of cognition toward that object.
3.3: Absorption (samādhi) is when the object alone shines forth, as if devoid of self-referentiality.
3.4: Together, these three—dhāraṇā, dhyāna, and samādhi—are saṁyama (integrated discipline).
3.5: By mastery of saṁyama, there is the light of knowledge (prajñā).
3.7: These three are more internal than the preceding two limbs (pratyāhāra and prāṇāyāma).
3.8: Yet even they are external with respect to seedless samādhi (nirbīja).
3.9: By restraining arising tendencies and cultivating the impression of restraint (nirodha-saṁskāra), transformation toward steadiness occurs (nirodha-pariṇāma).
3.10: With the subsidence of fluctuations, the mind reaches tranquility (praśānta-vāhitā).
3.11: In samādhi-transformation, distraction (vyutthāna) diminishes and one-pointedness (ekāgratā) arises.
3.12: In one-pointedness-transformation, the same thought recurs, becoming continuous (ekasaṁskāra).
3.13: From these are explained transformations of characteristics (dharma), state (lakṣaṇa), and condition (avasthā).
3.14: A substrate (dharmī) underlies all states and their transformations.
3.15: Different sequences of transformations are explained by differences in cause and effect (nimitta/pariṇāma).
3.16: By saṁyama on the threefold transformations, knowledge of past and future.
3.17: By saṁyama on sound—its meaning and idea—arises knowledge of another’s thought (śabda-artha-pratyayānām itaretarādhyāsāt saṁkaraḥ).
3.18: By direct perception of latent impressions (saṁskāra), knowledge of previous births.
3.19: By cognition of mental images (pratyayas), knowledge of another’s mind (citta).
3.20: But not of the underlying factors (adhikaraṇa), for those are not the object of saṁyama.
3.21: By saṁyama on the form of the body, suspending its perceptibility, the yogin becomes invisible (tanu-mātra).
3.22: Karmic fruition is fast or slow; by saṁyama on it, knowledge of death and its portents.
3.23: By saṁyama on friendliness, compassion, and joy, those qualities are obtained.
3.24: By saṁyama on strength of elephants and the like, strength is acquired.
3.25: By saṁyama on the inner light (jyotiḥ), knowledge of the subtle, hidden, and distant.
3.26: By saṁyama on the sun (sūrye), knowledge of the worlds (bhuvana).
3.27: By saṁyama on the moon (candre), knowledge of the arrangement of the stars.
3.28: By saṁyama on the pole-star (dhruva), knowledge of their motions.
3.29: By saṁyama on the navel center (nābhi-cakra), knowledge of the constitution of the body.
3.30: By saṁyama on the throat (kaṇṭha-kuhā), cessation of hunger and thirst.
3.31: By saṁyama on the tortoise channel (kūrma-nāḍī), steadiness.
3.32: By saṁyama on the light in the head, vision of perfected beings (siddha-darśana).
3.33: Or knowledge of everything arises from intuition (hṛdaye).
3.34: By saṁyama on the heart (hṛdaya), knowledge of the mind (citta).
3.35: Experience (bhoga) arises from failure to distinguish between the Seer (puruṣa) and sattva; by saṁyama on the Self, knowledge of Puruṣa and independence (kaivalya).
3.36: Thence arise superhuman hearing, touch, sight, taste, and smell (indriya-jaya).
3.37: These are obstacles to samādhi (for the contemplative), though powers in worldly states.
3.38: When attachment to the body’s cause is loosened, the mind can enter another’s body.
3.39: By mastery of the upward life-breath (udāna), one may rise above water, mud, and thorns, etc.
3.40: By saṁyama on the relation of ear and ether (ākāśa), divine hearing (śrotra-ākāśayoḥ saṁbandha).
3.41: By saṁyama on the relation of body and ether, lightness like cotton and passage through space.
3.42: By saṁyama on thought-processes (citta-pratyaya), knowledge of previous states (including births).
3.43: By saṁyama on the elements (bhūta)—their gross, subtle, inherent, and purposive forms—mastery over the elements (bhūta-jaya).
3.44: From that, minuteness (aṇimā), greatness (mahimā), lightness (laghimā), heaviness (garimā), reach (prāpti), and command (vaśitva) over them.
3.45: Perfection of the body: beauty, grace, strength, and adamantine firmness (vajra-samhananatā).
3.46: By saṁyama on the senses (indriya)—their forms and mastery—control over them and speed of mind.
3.47: From that, mastery over the primordial (prakṛti); independence of the senses and mind.
3.48: For one established in the Self, mastery over all states.
3.49: By detachment even from that, the seed of bondage is destroyed; omniscience and omnipotence begin to awaken.
3.50: When addressed by high beings (lords of creation), through dispassion there is no attachment or pride.
3.51: By discriminating between sattva and Puruṣa, there is sovereignty over all and knowledge of all—then freedom.
3.52: If invited by subtle beings, avoid attachment, or knowledge and elevation may be lost.
3.53: By saṁyama on moments (kṣaṇa) and their sequence, discriminative knowledge arises.
3.54: From that arises knowledge that distinguishes even similar objects by their subtle differences.
3.55: Supreme discriminative knowledge is instantaneous, all-comprehensive, and successive.
3.56: When the purity of sattva equals that of Puruṣa, there is isolation (kaivalya).
4.1: Perfections (siddhi) arise from birth, herbs, mantra, austerity (tapas), or samādhi.
4.2: Transformation into another species occurs by fulfillment of nature’s causes (prakṛti-āpūrti).
4.3: Incidental causes (nimitta) assist but do not produce the transformation; like a farmer opening a channel for water.
4.4: Minds (citta) are many, arising from differentiated egoism (asmitā).
4.5: Although activities vary, one mind is distinguished by its latent impressions (saṁskāra).
4.6: For minds born of meditation (dhyāna-jam), there is no sowing of karmic seeds (karmāśaya).
4.7: The actions of the yogin are neither white nor black; those of others are threefold (white, black, mixed).
4.8: From those actions, only impressions arise that fit their causes.
4.9: Because of sameness of memory and impressions, the flow continues even across births.
4.10: Impressions (vāsanā) have no beginning; the impulse to life is beginningless.
4.11: They are held together by cause, effect, substratum, and support; when these cease, the impressions cease.
4.12: Past and future exist in their own forms; differences of sequence are in the properties (dharma).
4.13: The properties manifest as specific (visesa), non-specific (avisesa), and balanced (sāmānya).
4.14: The essence of a thing (dravya) is an underlying field of properties (dharma).
4.15: Though the object is the same, it is seen differently according to the mind.
4.16: An object does not depend on a single mind; otherwise its destruction would follow that mind’s cessation.
4.17: The object is known or unknown depending on whether the mind is colored by it (citta-anukāra).
4.18: The mind is observable by the unchanging Seer (puruṣa).
4.19: The mind is not self-luminous (na sva-saṁvedana), for it is an object (dṛśya).
4.20: One mind cannot grasp both itself and the object simultaneously (ekasmin samaye).
4.21: If one mind were known by another, infinite regress would ensue.
4.22: When the mind takes the form of both Seer and seen, it understands its own nature.
4.23: The mind, colored by Seer and seen, is all-comprehending.
4.24: Though variegated by countless tendencies, the mind exists for another (puruṣa)—it is for-the-sake-of (arthavattva).
4.25: For one who has seen the distinction (viveka), the mind ceases to act as the cause of conjunction.
4.26: Then the mind tends toward discrimination continuously and moves toward dissolution (prasaṅkhyāna-pravāha).
4.27: Even then, due to past impressions, distractions may arise; because of them there is obstruction by residual afflictions.
4.28: They are removed in the same way as the afflictions were removed.
4.29: For one uninterested even in omniscience, established in discrimination, there is seedless samādhi (dharma-megha-samādhi, the ‘cloud of dharma’).
4.30: From dispassion even toward that, all residual afflictions end.
4.31: Then, with the coverings removed, knowledge is infinite; little remains to be known.
4.32: Then the sequence of transformations of the guṇas comes to an end, having fulfilled their purpose.
4.33: Sequence (kramā) is the moment (kṣaṇa) recognized by the end of the preceding and the beginning of the following.
4.34: When the guṇas, no longer serving the Seer (puruṣa), return to their source, isolation/liberation (kaivalya) shines: the Seer abides in its own nature.
